traps.name: Scholar's Mate
traps.engineNote: Objectively bad for White if Black defends accurately (the early queen sortie loses time), but below club level the four-move mate scores constantly because beginners develop the f6-knight and forget f7 is only defended by the king.

mainLine.note: A natural developing move that fatally ignores the threat to f7.

refutation.note: The clean defense: the pawn hits the queen and shields f7. Black is then simply better, since White's queen has wasted moves.
refutation.assessment: Black is comfortable; White's premature queen raid costs tempo (roughly equal to slightly better for Black).

mainLine.note: With f7 collapsing, the queen mates instantly. The bishop on c4 guards the escape, so it is mate.

traps.name: Legal Mate
traps.engineNote: A genuinely sound queen sacrifice once Black has committed the pinning bishop to g4 and a passive setup; the resulting mating net is forced if Black grabs the queen.

mainLine.note: The point: the knight 'hangs' the queen on d1, but taking it is fatal because of the bishop on c4 and the knight on c3.
Greedily snatching the queen — but the bishop leaves the defense of the kingside.

refutation.note: Declining: recapture the knight instead of grabbing the queen. Black keeps the queen-for-knight imbalance favorably and avoids the mate.
refutation.assessment: After 6.Qxg4 Black is fine and is up a pawn-equivalent; the trap only works if the queen is taken.

mainLine.note: The first hammer blow: the bishop checks and drags the king out.
Forced — the king cannot go to f8 or d7 cleanly into the net.
The knight delivers mate; the king is boxed in by its own pieces and the two White minors.

traps.name: Tennison Gambit Trap
traps.engineNote: The gambit itself is dubious objectively, but once Black accepts the pawn and then casually kicks the g5-knight, the f7 double-sacrifice is a forced queen win that beginners walk into often.

mainLine.note: The instinctive 'kick the knight' move — but it loses on the spot to a double sacrifice on f7 and g6.

refutation.note: Solid: blunt the bishop's diagonal and prepare to develop. Now White has nothing concrete for the gambit pawn.
refutation.assessment: After a quiet retreat Black is at least equal and holds the extra pawn.

mainLine.note: The first sacrifice rips open the king.
Practically forced — declining drops material with the king still exposed.
The second sacrifice: the bishop deflects the king off the e8-d8 line.
Forced; the king must take or step into a mating net.
The whole point: with the king dragged to g6, White's queen scoops the undefended black queen on d8 for two minor pieces — a decisive material win.

traps.name: London System Poisoned b2
traps.engineNote: Sound and practical: after Nc3 the b2-pawn is a real trap. Grabbing it lets Na4 hit the queen, and the queen is rounded up for a knight and a pawn.

mainLine.note: The poisoned pawn: snatching b2 brings the queen deep into White's camp with no escape route.

refutation.note: Develop and leave b2 alone. Black is fine; the b2-pawn was bait, not a free meal.
refutation.assessment: A normal London middlegame, roughly balanced.

mainLine.note: The knight attacks the queen and cuts off its retreat squares.
The only check, hoping to wriggle free — but it runs into c3.
Blocks the check and attacks the queen again; now the queen can only take the knight.
Forced — every other queen move drops the queen outright.
White wins the queen for a knight and a pawn — a winning material advantage.

traps.name: Blackburne Shilling Gambit
traps.engineNote: Objectively dubious (White is better with the calm 4.Nxe5 then careful play, or simply 4.c3), but the greedy 4.Nxe5?? is exactly the move a beginner plays, and then Qg5 wins on the spot.

mainLine.note: Grabbing the 'free' e5-pawn — the move the whole gambit is designed to provoke.

refutation.note: Don't take the bait: kick the d4-knight first. White keeps a healthy center and Black's knight tour has only lost time.
refutation.assessment: After 4.c3 Nxf3+ 5.Qxf3 White is simply better with a strong center and no tactics to fear.

mainLine.note: The double attack: the queen hits both the e5-knight and the g2-pawn. White cannot save both.
A greedy fork attempt on the queen and rook — but it ignores Black's attack.
Black takes g2 and threatens the rook on h1 plus mating ideas around f3.
Saving the rook, but the king is now stuck in the center.
Grabbing the e4-pawn with check, dragging White's defense apart.
Blocking the check — but it walks into the smothered-style finish.
The d4-knight delivers mate; the king is hemmed in by its own pieces.

traps.name: Fishing Pole Trap
traps.engineNote: Offering the g4-knight is objectively a small concession, but if White actually grabs it the open h-file plus the en-passant break on f3 give Black a near-forced mating attack that scores heavily below master level.

variations.label: 6.hxg4?? hxg4 7.Nxe5 Qh4 8.f4 gxf3 e.p. 9.Nxf7 f2+ 10.Rxf2 Qh1#

mainLine.note: Taking the knight is the fatal greed: it rips open the h-file straight at White's castled king.

refutation.note: Don't take the bait — retreat the knight and leave the g4-knight alone. White stays safe and keeps a normal Ruy Lopez.
refutation.assessment: After the calm retreat Black has nothing; White is comfortable.

mainLine.note: Recapturing opens the h-file with the rook already bearing down on h1.
Snatching another pawn and ignoring the storm — typical of the player who took the first bait.
The queen swings to h4 with the deadly threat of Qh1#/Qh2# down the open file.
Trying to give the king the f2-square — but it allows a crushing en-passant capture.
Capturing en passant: the g4-pawn takes the f4-pawn's passing square on f3, prying open the king.
A last desperate grab; nothing saves White now.
Pushing the pawn with check, dragging the rook to a fatal square.
Forced, but it vacates the first rank.
The queen mates on h1; the king has no escape down the file it opened itself.

traps.name: Stafford Gambit
traps.engineNote: Objectively a touch worse for Black with best play (White holds with 4.Nc4! or careful development), but the practical traps are vicious: the greedy 4.Nxc6 leads to a king hunt or a queen win, and beginners stumble into both.

mainLine.note: Returning the knight to grab another piece looks natural but hands Black a huge development lead and open lines.

refutation.note: The engine refutation: retreat the knight to c4, keeping the extra pawn while sidestepping every trap.
refutation.assessment: After 4.Nc4 White is objectively a little better and avoids all the tricks.

mainLine.note: Recapturing opens the d-file and the c8-bishop's diagonal for a fast attack.
A natural-looking developing move that does nothing about Black's initiative.
Aiming the bishop at f2 — the soft spot in White's uncastled position.
Pinning the f6-knight, but the pin is an illusion: the knight jumps with devastating effect.
Breaking the 'pin' — if White grabs the queen, Black mates first.
Taking the queen, walking straight into mate.
The bishop checks and pulls the king into the open.
Forced; the king has nowhere safe.
The light-squared bishop completes the mating net — the king is trapped by Black's minor pieces.
Again the greedy capture — but here White's later h3 lets Black win the queen instead of mating.

refutation.note: Retreat with 4.Nc4! and the whole gambit fizzles; White keeps the pawn and stays safe.
refutation.assessment: White is slightly better after 4.Nc4 with no tactics to fear.

mainLine.note: Opening lines for the rapid attack.
Passive; Black's pieces flow out faster.
Targeting f2 once more.
A wasted waiting move that fails to address the e4/f2 tactics.
The knight sacrifice opens the d-file toward White's queen.
Recapturing — but now the f2-bishop fork wins the queen.
A zwischenzug check before collecting the queen.
Forced recapture; the king steps off the back rank.
Black scoops the queen on d1 for a bishop — a decisive material advantage.

traps.name: Elephant Trap
traps.engineNote: A classic equalizer: the d5-knight grab looks like it wins a pawn against the pinned f6-knight, but the in-between checks win a clean piece for Black.

mainLine.note: Grabbing the d5-pawn 'because the f6-knight is pinned' — the entire point of the trap.

refutation.note: First resolve the pin: capture on f6, and only then take on d5. That way White safely keeps an even game.
refutation.assessment: After 6.Bxf6 Nxf6 7.e3 it is a normal, balanced Queen's Gambit Declined.

mainLine.note: The knight wasn't really pinned — taking back exposes the bishop on g5 and the queen behind it.
Winning the queen, but Black's in-between check turns the tables.
The key zwischenzug: check first, before recapturing the bishop on d8.
Blocking the check with the queen — the only way to meet it.
Trading off, still with check, keeping the initiative.
Forced recapture.
Black finally collects the bishop on d8 and emerges a clean minor piece up for a pawn.

traps.name: Englund Gambit Mate
traps.engineNote: The gambit is objectively poor, but the queen raid to b2 sets a beautiful mating trap; if White defends the rook with the natural Qd2 instead of Bxb4, it is mate in two.

mainLine.note: Defending the c3-bishop and the a1-rook in one move looks sensible — but it allows a forced mate.

refutation.note: The only good move: capture the b4-bishop instead. White survives, down just a pawn rather than mated.
refutation.assessment: After 7.Bxb4 Nxb4 White has weathered the storm and is only slightly worse.

mainLine.note: Removing the defender of the d2-queen and clearing the way to c1.
Forced recapture — but the back rank is now fatally weak.
The queen drops to c1 with mate; the king is boxed in on its own first rank.

traps.name: Lasker Trap
traps.engineNote: Famous for an early underpromotion: if White grabs the b4-bishop, the pawn promotes to a knight with check, and Black wins the queen. The Albin is rare at beginner level, but the trick is unforgettable.

mainLine.note: Snatching the b4-bishop overlooks that the e3-pawn is about to wreck White's king.

refutation.note: Recapture the e3-pawn first, keeping the king safe. White avoids the whole combination.
refutation.assessment: After 6.fxe3 Bxd2+ 7.Nxd2 the position is roughly balanced; the trap never fires.

mainLine.note: The pawn crashes through with check, exposing White's king.
Stepping out — but now the underpromotion is decisive.
The signature underpromotion: promoting to a knight gives check, where a new queen would not. This wins time to grab White's queen.
Taking the knight, but it abandons the d1-queen's defense along the back rank.
Check from the c8-bishop, skewering the king and the queen.
Forced; every king move leaves the queen hanging.
Black collects the queen — a winning material edge after the famous underpromotion.

traps.name: Noah's Ark Trap
traps.engineNote: A timeless trap: if White grabs the e5-pawn with the queen and then chases with Qd5, Black's pawns roll forward and snare the b3-bishop with a3-b5-c4 cage.

variations.label: 8.Qxd4?? c5 9.Qd5 Be6 10.Qc6+ Bd7 11.Qd5 c4 (the bishop is trapped)

mainLine.note: Recapturing the pawn with the queen looks fine, but the queen will be chased into a fatal square while the b3-bishop gets caged.

refutation.note: Keep the bishop active and safe with Bd5; do not commit the queen to the chase. White stays solid.
refutation.assessment: After 8.Bd5 c6 9.Bb3 the bishop slips back to safety and the trap is avoided.

mainLine.note: Gaining a tempo on the queen and starting the pawn net around the b3-bishop.
Centralizing the queen — but it walks into a string of tempo-gaining hits.
Attacking the queen again and preparing to fence in the bishop.
A check, but it only helps Black complete the development of the trap.
Blocking the check with tempo, kicking the queen back.
Retreating — but now the decisive pawn push lands.
Slamming the cage shut: the b3-bishop has only a4 or c4, and both are captured by a pawn.
The only try to save the bishop — but it lands on a square a pawn covers.
Black captures the trapped bishop and emerges with a winning material advantage.
